headline: Jump's AI Meeting Platform Reaches 27,000 Advisors as $80M Series B Brings Total to $105M
summary: Jump's AI platform now processes advisor-client meetings end-to-end — prep, transcription, CRM updates, task generation, and compliance documentation — across 27,000 financial advisors, a figure the company states represents approximately 1 in 10 US advisors (per Jump's own press release via Business Wire; the implied denominator of ~270,000 aligns with Cerulli-range estimates of practicing advisors but is not independently sourced here). The company has processed what it describes as 183 continuous years of client meeting content and scaled from 3 to 200 employees in under two years (both figures per Jump's Business Wire announcement). The $80M Series B, led by Insight Partners with strategic participation from Allianz Life Ventures, TIAA Ventures, and Citi Ventures, brings total funding to $105M and embeds Jump into the distribution strategies of major insurance and retirement incumbents — not just the advisor workflow layer. The investor composition — a top-tier software growth fund plus product manufacturers — suggests Jump is being capitalized to become infrastructure between advisors and the firms that supply them, not merely a productivity tool.
the_so_what: Jump claims 27,000 advisors versus Zocks' self-reported 5,000+ firms (per Zocks' own marketing materials referenced in our landscape data), a roughly 5x gap in distribution footprint that compounds as Jump layers active workflow execution — CRM writes, email drafting, planning-system actions — that neither Zocks nor Holistiplan have publicly matched. Any wealth management firm running a build-vs-buy evaluation on advisor AI workflow automation now needs to weigh whether a proprietary effort can close a data advantage built on ~10% of US advisor conversations and backed by $105M in capital, or whether partnering with the emerging default platform is the faster path.

key_stat.label: financial advisors on Jump's AI platform (per Jump's Business Wire press release)

capability_evidence.evidence: Jump states it processes advisor-client meetings end-to-end across 27,000 advisors, covering prep, notes, CRM updates, tasks, and compliance docs, with 183 continuous years of meeting content processed (Business Wire press release, Feb 2026).
capability_evidence.metric: 27,000 advisors; 183 continuous years of meeting content processed

entities.key_topic: AI operating system Series B funding

_research.whats_new: Company has 2 past entries. Most recent: "Jump Moves Beyond Insights to Execution: AI Associate Acts Across CRM, Email and Planning Systems in Real Time" (2026-03-26). Current advisor_productivity maturity: scaled. Current headline: "Jump processes advisor-client meetings end-to-end: prep, notes, CRM updates, tasks, and compliance docs — at scale across 27,000 advisors". Compare against this story to identify what is genuinely new.

_iterations.headline: Jump's AI Operating System Reaches 27,000 Advisors as $80M Series B Brings Total Funding to $105M
_iterations.the_so_what: At 27,000 advisors, Jump now has roughly 5x the firm footprint of closest meeting-AI competitor Zocks (5,000+ firms) and is layering execution capabilities that Zocks and Holistiplan have not publicly matched — creating a data and distribution moat that grows harder to close with each quarter. Any wealth management firm evaluating build-vs-buy for advisor AI workflow automation now faces the question of whether their proprietary effort can match a platform that already ingests ~10% of US advisor conversations and is backed by the capital to keep compounding that advantage.

specificity.feedback: Headline contains specific metric (27,000 advisors), specific funding amount ($80M Series B), and total funding ($105M). Strong.

so_what.feedback: References Zocks by name with a specific 5x footprint comparison, references Holistiplan by name on capability gap, and makes a falsifiable claim about data/distribution moat. The build-vs-buy framing is directly actionable for the target audience. This would hold up in a board discussion.

source.feedback: The '183 continuous years of meetings' figure, the '3 employees to 200' growth claim, and critically the '~1 in 10 US advisors' denominator all need source verification. What is the total US advisor count being used — 270,000? That matters because BLS, FINRA, and Cerulli give different numbers (roughly 300K-330K registered reps vs. ~270K-280K practicing advisors). The Zocks '5,000+ firms' figure also needs a named source — is that from Zocks' own materials or an estimate? If any of these are inferred from press release language or rounded, flag it. Six sources are cited but none are named in the entry itself.

substance.feedback: The investor mix analysis (Insight Partners + Allianz/TIAA/Citi strategic investors as signal of positioning as infrastructure vs. point tool) is genuine synthesis that goes well beyond the press release. The expansion from passive meeting intelligence into active workflow execution is a meaningful analytical observation. The '183 continuous years' data volume point contextualizes scale effectively.

stat.feedback: 27,000 advisors with the ~1-in-10 contextualization is decision-grade. A CXO can use this in a board deck to frame market penetration of a potential vendor or competitive threat. The parenthetical gives it the denominator context needed.

competitor.feedback: Names Zocks with a specific 5x footprint comparison and Holistiplan on capability gap. Both are tracked peers in the landscape. Strong competitive framing.

evaluation.priority_fix: Source traceability: The '~1 in 10 US advisors' claim depends on an unstated denominator that varies materially by data source (BLS, FINRA, Cerulli all differ). Name the denominator source and total used. The Zocks '5,000+ firms' comparison also needs a cited source — if it's from Zocks' own website, say so; if it's an estimate, caveat it. A $5,000/year client should never have to wonder whether our competitive comparisons are sourced or inferred.

_iterations.headline: Jump's AI Meeting Platform Reaches 27,000 Advisors as $80M Series B Brings Total to $105M
_iterations.the_so_what: Jump claims 27,000 advisors versus Zocks' self-reported 5,000+ firms (per Zocks' own marketing materials referenced in our landscape data), a roughly 5x gap in distribution footprint that compounds as Jump layers active workflow execution — CRM writes, email drafting, planning-system actions — that neither Zocks nor Holistiplan have publicly matched. Any wealth management firm running a build-vs-buy evaluation on advisor AI workflow automation now needs to weigh whether a proprietary effort can close a data advantage built on ~10% of US advisor conversations and backed by $105M in capital, or whether partnering with the emerging default platform is the faster path.

specificity.feedback: Headline includes both a specific metric (27,000 advisors) and funding detail ($80M Series B, $105M total). Clear deployment scope.

so_what.feedback: The so_what makes a falsifiable competitive claim — a ~5x distribution gap over Zocks, names Holistiplan as lacking matched capabilities, and frames a specific build-vs-buy decision against a quantified data moat (~10% of US advisor conversations, $105M capital). Removing the company name, the claim about workflow execution layering on top of distribution footprint as a compounding advantage still holds as an industry-level thesis.

source.feedback: The entry is unusually disciplined on sourcing. Every key number — 27,000 advisors, 183 years of content, 3-to-200 employees, $80M/$105M funding — is explicitly attributed to Jump's Business Wire press release. The 1-in-10 claim is flagged as Jump's own framing with the caveat that the implied ~270K denominator aligns with Cerulli-range estimates but is not independently verified. Zocks' 5,000+ figure is attributed to Zocks' own marketing materials. This is above the bar.

substance.feedback: The summary goes well beyond restating the press release. The investor composition analysis — decomposing Insight Partners as a software growth fund versus Allianz Life Ventures, TIAA Ventures, and Citi Ventures as product manufacturers — and the inference that Jump is being positioned as infrastructure between advisors and their suppliers, not just a productivity tool, is genuine analytical value-add. The 3-to-200 headcount growth contextualizes execution velocity.

stat.feedback: 27,000 financial advisors is decision-grade. A CXO can put this number on a slide with the source attribution provided. It answers the question 'how big is this platform?' directly.

competitor.feedback: Names Zocks with a specific quantified comparison (5,000+ firms vs. 27,000 advisors, ~5x gap) and names Holistiplan as lacking matched workflow execution capabilities. Both are tracked peers in the landscape.
